text: The laparoscopic view shows the starting point of the bladder dissection, with previously dissected corneal structures visible. The surgeon prepares to begin dissecting the scarred bladder tissue.

The surgeon begins the laparoscopic dissection of the bladder, focusing on the lateral window where the bladder is not adherent. The video shows the initial steps of the dissection, highlighting anatomical landmarks and potential pitfalls in the scarred area due to previous caesarean sections.

The surgeon begins by separating the anterior and posterior leaf of the broad ligament, aiming for an avascular dissection to maintain a clear field of vision. The UV fold is incised below the scar tissue layer, and the loose areolar tissue is gently dissected away from the lateral window, positioned just below the UV fold and above the uterine artery.

The surgeon performs gentle blunt dissection in a space relatively free of adhesions, revealing the uterine artery as the first landmark. The bare surface of the anterior cervix is also visible during this dissection.

The uterine artery is clearly visible, serving as the first landmark for the dissection. The surgeon lifts the UV fold and makes small, gentle cuts close to the uterus to separate the UV fold. If the UV fold is loosely attached, it is dissected entirely before proceeding further.

The surgeon identifies the junction of scar tissue with the uterus and proceeds with dissection. The bare cervix is visible, minimizing the risk of encountering the bladder. The plane between the UV fold of peritoneum and the bladder is visible, allowing for incision of the UV fold to enhance bladder visibility. Additional stretch is applied to the uterus from the vaginal end to facilitate dissection of the UV fold, staying above the marked scar tissue area to safely dissect the bladder.

The surgeon incises the UV fold of peritoneum from left to right, extending to the right round ligament. With the UV fold dissected, the bladder fascia becomes visible, allowing further dissection. The dissection proceeds from the left side to the right side, revealing fat tissue associated with the bladder, which is kept superior to the dissection plane.

The surgeon develops the space on the right side by cutting the UV fold, then returns to the left side to dissect the bladder. Excess blood is suctioned out to prevent staining of the loose areolar tissue.

The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of the bladder, applying traction from the vaginal end to maintain tension and separation from the anterior cervix. The cervix appears white and firm, while the bladder is softer. The uterine artery is visible, marked in red, and scar tissue is marked in blue. The correct plane of dissection is indicated by an orange arrow. The surgeon carefully incises the scar tissue at its junction to the uterus, avoiding the bladder, which is positioned below the cervix.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the bladder close to the cervix, using a manipulator to push the uterus upward. The bladder is kept under stretch with one hand, while the surgeon gently lifts it up, noting the slight color difference between the cervix and bladder. The movement of the bladder and cervix helps identify the correct plane of dissection, proceeding at least up to the midline.

The surgeon reaches the midline and begins operating from the opposite side, connecting the established plane of dissection to the other side. The bladder is stretched using a grasper, while the uterus is manipulated to delineate between the bladder and the lower uterine segment. The uterus is shifted to the patient's right side, and the surgeon lifts the bladder, performing blunt dissection in the loose areolar tissue to locate the uterine artery.

The surgeon gently tunnels through the loose posterior peritoneum, incising it to locate the uterine artery and vein at the lateral border of the cervix. The uterine artery is identified below the grasper, while the bladder is visible above it. The anterior surface of the cervix appears glistening and is distinguished by its firmer consistency compared to the softer bladder tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects the loose areolar tissue to expose the anterior surface of the cervix, with the bladder positioned above. The dissection is performed close to the cervix to push the bladder away, while the assistant maintains tension on the bladder from the contralateral side.

The surgeon uses traction provided by the assistant to dissect anterior to the uterine artery, revealing the white glistening surface of the cervix. The bladder scar tissue is incised close to the cervix to push the bladder down, using small bites with a harmonic scalpel or bipolar vessel sealer and cutter.

The surgeon identifies the correct plane of dissection, revealing the white cervix and anteriorly running uterine vessels. Loose areolar tissue is visible in the lateral space. The surgeon grasps the bladder with a grasper and makes gentle cuts on the anterior surface of the cervix, ensuring careful dissection to avoid bleeding. The lateral space is clearly developed, allowing for bold cutting of the remaining scar tissue and pushing the bladder down.

The surgeon gently dissects the bladder in small bits, gradually moving it away to clear the field. With the dissection planes developed on both sides, the uterus is pushed cranially and kept in the midline to expose the tongue-shaped bladder tissue adherent to the anterior cervix. The surgeon confidently cuts this tissue, using a grasper in the left hand to lift the bladder scar tissue and a right-handed instrument to make gentle cuts, effectively pushing the bladder away.

The surgeon demonstrates the dissection of the bladder away from the cervix, using a bipolar sealer and cutter. The bladder is seen moving safely away from both sides, revealing the uterine artery. The posterior peritoneum is gently lifted and incised to expose the uterine vessels, facilitating further surgical progress.
